Bring your own agents, assign goals, and track your agents' work and costs from one dashboard.

parts:
Here’s the cleanest way to think about it **for agent workflows**:

## Bottom line

**Linear** is best if you already run work in Linear and want agents embedded into your existing PM system.  
**Paperclip** is best if you want a **control plane for many heterogeneous agents** with governance, budgets, org charts, and auditability.  
**Multica.ai** is best if your main use case is **coding agents acting like teammates** on software tasks. citeturn176032view0turn176032view1turn176032view2turn176032view4turn176032view3turn912495view0

## Fast compare

| Tool | What it is | Best for | Weak spot |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Linear** | Project/work management platform with built-in agent support and MCP access | Teams already using Linear for product/engineering work | Less of a full “agent operating system” than the others citeturn176032view0turn176032view1 |
| **Paperclip** | Open-source orchestration/control plane for teams of agents | Running many agents across roles, providers, and runtimes with governance | Overkill for a small team or a single coding agent citeturn176032view2turn176032view4 |
| **Multica.ai** | Open-source AI-native task management for coding agents | Software teams using Claude Code/Codex as active contributors | More specialized around coding than cross-functional business ops citeturn176032view3turn912495view0 |

## What each one means in practice

### 1) Linear
Linear treats agents as workspace “app users.” You can assign issues to them, @mention them in comments/descriptions, and provide guidance they automatically receive while working. Importantly, the human teammate still owns the issue even after delegating work to an agent. citeturn176032view0

Why that matters:
- Great when your team already lives in Linear.
- You get agent behavior inside normal issue/project workflows instead of adopting a separate system.
- Linear also has an **MCP server** so external AI clients like Claude, Cursor, and Codex can find/create/update Linear objects through a standard interface. citeturn176032view1

Good signs:
- Strong ecosystem and integrations directory. citeturn397873search2
- Agent features extend into Slack, triage automation, Intercom, Zendesk, etc. citeturn397873search3turn397873search5turn397873search7turn397873search8

Use Linear when:
- You want **agent-native issue tracking**.
- Your question is “how do I plug agents into our existing delivery workflow?”

Do not pick Linear first when:
- You want to run a whole multi-agent company with budgets, hierarchical delegation, and board-style governance.

### 2) Paperclip
Paperclip’s own framing is helpful: it is **not** a chatbot, not an agent framework, and not a workflow builder. It is a **control plane** for organizing many agents into a company structure, with org charts, goals, budgets, governance, heartbeats, ticketing, and audit trails. It is open source, MIT-licensed, self-hosted, and supports bring-your-own agents/runtimes. citeturn176032view4turn176032view2

What makes it different:
- **Org-chart model** instead of task-board-first model. citeturn176032view4
- **Heartbeats** let agents wake on schedules and act. citeturn176032view4
- **Budgets and auto-pause** help control runaway spend. citeturn176032view4
- **Governance/approval gates/rollback** are built into the product story. citeturn176032view4
- Supports many types of workers: Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, shell, HTTP, etc. citeturn176032view2turn176032view4

Use Paperclip when:
- You have multiple agents with different roles.
- You care about **control, accountability, cost ceilings, and structure** more than simple task assignment.
- You want something closer to an “agent operations center.”

Do not pick Paperclip first when:
- Your main need is just “assign coding tasks to Claude/Codex and watch progress.”
- You only have one or two agents.

### 3) Multica.ai
Multica is explicitly aimed at **coding agents as teammates**. Agents show up in the assignee picker beside humans, can claim work, update status, report blockers, comment, and stream progress in real time. It supports Claude Code and OpenAI Codex out of the box, offers cloud and self-hosted modes, and emphasizes runtimes plus reusable “skills.” citeturn176032view3turn912495view0

What stands out:
- Very strong **engineering-task lifecycle**: enqueue → claim → start → complete/fail. citeturn912495view0
- **Real-time execution visibility** with tool-call/progress streaming. citeturn912495view0
- **Reusable skills** are a core concept, not an add-on. citeturn176032view3turn912495view0
- **Runtime layer** is central: local daemon or cloud runtime, auto-detecting installed CLIs like Claude Code/Codex. citeturn176032view3turn912495view0
- Says execution happens on your machine or your own infra rather than code passing through Multica servers. citeturn912495view0

Use Multica when:
- You want a **software-engineering manager for coding agents**.
- You care about watching runs, blockers, task state, and accumulating coding skills.

Do not pick Multica first when:
- You want non-engineering agents across sales/marketing/research/ops with company-style delegation and budgets.

## My practical verdict

If you mean **“best tool inside an agentic software team”**:

- **Pick Linear** if you already use Linear and want the lowest-friction adoption.
- **Pick Multica** if the center of gravity is **coding execution**.
- **Pick Paperclip** if the center of gravity is **multi-agent orchestration and governance**.

## Simple recommendation by scenario

**Scenario A: existing startup using Linear + GitHub + Slack**  
Go with **Linear** first. It already supports agents, Slack interaction, triage automation, and MCP-based access from external AI clients. It is the easiest incremental step. citeturn176032view0turn176032view1turn397873search3turn397873search5

**Scenario B: team wants Claude Code/Codex to work tickets autonomously**  
Go with **Multica**. That is almost exactly its product pitch and architecture. citeturn176032view3turn912495view0

**Scenario C: you want a fleet of agents across many business functions**  
Go with **Paperclip**. It is built around organizational hierarchy, delegation, budgets, governance, and audits. citeturn176032view4turn176032view2

## One-line summary

- **Linear = agent-enabled work management**
- **Multica = agent-native coding task management**
- **Paperclip = agent company control plane** citeturn176032view0turn176032view3turn176032view4

I can also turn this into a **buyer-style matrix** with rows like setup effort, observability, governance, integrations, coding depth, and best team size.

{"plan": {"plan_id": "plan-2026-04-08T08:00:00Z-agent-tools-compare", "version": 4, "title": "Agent tools comparison", "steps": [{"id": "step-1", "text": "Collect official product pages for Linear, Paperclip, Multica, and Craft Agents.", "status": "completed", "reason": "Initial comparative summary and fast compare were provided earlier; collection of primary product pages has been started and the conversation moved on to deeper extraction tasks."}, {"id": "step-2", "text": "Extract feature matrices and architecture details from docs and GitHub repos.", "status": "in_progress", "reason": "Assistant is actively opening and extracting details from official docs and GitHub repos for Linear, Paperclip, Multica, and Craft Agents (searches and opens performed; detailed extraction ongoing)."}, {"id": "step-3", "text": "Identify similar open-source and commercial agent platforms and list them.", "status": "pending", "reason": null}, {"id": "step-4", "text": "Compare costs, deployment models, governance, and integrations across tools.", "status": "pending", "reason": null}, {"id": "step-5", "text": "Summarize strengths, weaknesses, and recommended use cases for each tool.", "status": "pending", "reason": null}]}, "step_statuses_by_plan": {"plan-2026-04-08T08:00:00Z-agent-tools-compare": {"step-1": "completed", "step-2": "in_progress", "step-3": "pending", "step-4": "pending", "step-5": "pending"}}, "waiting_for_user_response_on_plan_until": null, "status": "completed", "last_updated_at": "2026-04-08T02:20:22.596102Z", "research_started_at": "2026-04-08T01:55:58.166780Z", "research_stopped_at": "2026-04-08T02:20:22.595820Z", "report_message": {"id": "604ba62d-f690-4c41-bc0e-157cb139c20b", "author": {"role": "assistant", "name": null, "metadata": {"real_author": "tool:web"}}, "create_time": 1775614073.10064, "update_time": null, "content": {"content_type": "text", "parts": ["# Comparative Analysis of Agent Platforms: Linear Agents, Paperclip, Multica, and Craft Agents

## Executive summary

Across the four products, there are two fundamentally different “agent platform” archetypes: **PM-native agent enablement inside an existing SaaS system** (Linear) versus **agent-first control planes / runtimes** (Paperclip, Multica) and **agent-first desktop orchestration interfaces** (Craft Agents). Linear’s approach treats agents as **workspace members** and emphasizes workflow-native delegation, auditability, and a broad integration ecosystem—while still keeping administrative boundaries tight (agents can’t sign in or manage users). citeturn15view0turn27view1turn5view3

Paperclip and Multica invert the model: they provide a **control plane whose primary job is coordinating autonomous execution**. Paperclip is “company-layer orchestration” built around governance (approvals), budgets, a heartbeat execution contract, and immutable audit trails, with adapters to many agent runtimes (Claude Code, Codex, etc.). citeturn19view0turn17view5turn17view3turn21view1turn17view1turn27view2 Multica is closer to “Linear for coding agents”: a task/issue workspace where agents are first-class teammates, backed by a Go + WebSocket backend and a **local daemon runtime** that polls, executes, streams progress, and sends heartbeats. citeturn11view0turn18view0turn13view2

Craft Agents is best understood as an **agent-native desktop interface**: it connects to MCP servers, REST APIs, and local folders (“sources”), offers strong user-in-the-loop controls (Explore/Ask/Execute permission modes), automations, and encrypted local credential storage. Its strength is fast, flexible, user-driven integration—its limitation is that it’s primarily a personal/team desktop control surface rather than an enterprise governance control plane. citeturn16view0turn16view2turn16view1turn27view0turn5view13turn8view1

## Linear agents in Linear

### Purpose and positioning

Linear positions agents as **“Artificial teammates”** that can collaborate on complex work or take delegated issues “end-to-end,” while remaining native to the Linear workflow (assignment, project membership, @mentions). citeturn27view1 In Linear’s “AI Agents” documentation, agents are explicitly treated as **third-party integrations approved by the workspace** and are **not billable seats**. citeturn15view0

Notably, Linear draws a hard boundary around administrative risk: agents **cannot sign in**, **cannot access admin functionality**, and **cannot manage users**. citeturn15view0 This makes the product conceptually closer to “safe operational delegation inside a PM system,” not a generalized agent runtime.

**Primary sources (official):**
```text
https://linear.app/docs/agents-in-linear
https://linear.app/agents
https://linear.app/developers/agents
https://linear.app/docs/mcp
https://linear.app/docs/api-and-webhooks
```

### Architecture

Linear’s agent architecture is best viewed as **SaaS-first** with multiple standardized access paths:

* **Agent apps built as integrations**: Linear’s developer docs describe installing an agent into a workspace via OAuth2, and specifically call out “actor” mode (`actor=app`) so actions are performed by the app/agent identity rather than a human user token. Workspace-scope installation requires admin permissions. citeturn5view1turn15view4turn15view3  
* **GraphQL + webhooks**: Linear’s public API is GraphQL (the same API Linear uses internally), with full mutation support; webhooks push change notifications for integration logic. citeturn15view2turn15view6  
* **MCP server**: Linear provides an MCP server as a standardized interface allowing MCP-compatible agents/clients to access Linear data securely and directly (including native connections in tools like Claude and Cursor, per Linear docs). citeturn5view2turn15view9

### Integrations

Linear’s integration posture is broad and ecosystem-oriented:

* The **Integration Directory** contains Linear-built (“Linear crafted”) and third-party integrations; workspace admins typically install integrations. citeturn15view1  
* Linear’s pricing feature matrix calls out “**MCP access**” as part of its AI/agent workflows, and lists integrations such as **Zendesk and Intercom**, along with Slack-based intake workflows (e.g., “Slack intake”). citeturn15view9  
* Linear for Agents lists prebuilt agent offerings spanning coding and QA use cases (e.g., Codex, GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Sentry), implying a marketplace-like distribution model. citeturn27view1

**Coverage against requested integration categories (evidence-based):** PM: native (Linear). VCS: implied via “agents” like GitHub Copilot and agent partners converting issues to PRs. citeturn27view1 Chat/intake: Slack intake called out in plan matrix. citeturn15view9 Observability: audit log available (see next section). citeturn5view3 CI/CD: not explicitly documented in the captured sources as a first-class integration category (likely exists via third-party integrations, but treat as *not proven here*). citeturn15view1

### Governance and security

Linear’s governance/security features are primarily those of a mature SaaS platform plus extra agent boundaries:

* **Audit logs**: Linear tracks access/subscription/settings-related events (including IP and country), retains logs for 90 days, restricts access to workspace owners, and supports querying via the API. citeturn5view3  
* **Identity and access controls**: SCIM provisioning is available on Enterprise. citeturn15view7 Login methods can be restricted; SAML authentication and IP restrictions are Enterprise-only. citeturn15view8turn5view4  
* **Agent-specific limits**: Agents can’t sign in or do admin/user management work. citeturn15view0  
* **Data use stance**: Linear states it does not train on customer data; however, because agents are typically third-party integrations, Linear instructs customers to evaluate the agent provider’s practices separately. citeturn15view0

### Developer experience

Linear is arguably the strongest developer platform of the four for *PM-integrated agents* because it offers:

* A well-defined **GraphQL endpoint** and schema introspection. citeturn15view6  
* An official **TypeScript SDK** exposing the GraphQL schema as typed models and operations. citeturn15view5  
* OAuth guidance, including actor authorization to represent app/agent identities. citeturn15view3turn15view4

### Observability and debugging

Linear provides platform-level observability (audit logs, real-time mutation propagation). citeturn5view3turn15view2 For agent-level debugging, Linear for Agents emphasizes the ability to “inspect underlying reasoning” behind changes, but the exact format (full traces, tool-call logs) is not described in the official excerpts captured here. citeturn27view1

### Cost model and licensing

Linear is commercial SaaS priced per user per month (with annual billing shown in the pricing page excerpt): Basic and Business tiers are priced, Enterprise is custom. citeturn15view9 Importantly, **agents are not counted as billable seats**. citeturn15view0

### Setup and operational effort

Operationally, Linear is lowest-effort for teams already using Linear: installing agents is akin to installing integrations and authorizing access. citeturn15view1turn27view1 For building agents, developers must implement OAuth, decide on actor mode, and host their integration/agent service externally (as Linear is not self-hosted in any referenced official material). citeturn15view3turn15view4

### Best-fit use cases and key limitations

Linear is best when the primary goal is **agent-enabled product/engineering workflow automation** (triage, specs, “issue → PR” loops) inside a PM system that already manages work. citeturn27view1turn15view9 The key limitation is that you inherit the constraints of a PM-integrated identity model: it’s not a general-purpose runtime, and agent behavior heavily depends on the third-party agent provider’s capabilities and security stance. citeturn15view0

## Paperclip (paperclipai)

### Purpose and positioning

Paperclip positions itself as **“open-source orchestration for zero-human companies”**—a “company layer” that sits above multiple agents/runtimes and provides structure (org charts), governance, budgeting, and real-time tracking. citeturn19view0turn17view0turn27view2

The core value proposition, across docs and marketing, is that it behaves like a task manager but adds **company-grade operational controls**: approvals, costs, audit trails, and a persistent ticket system. citeturn17view0turn21view4turn27view2

**Primary sources (official):**
```text
https://github.com/paperclipai/paperclip
https://docs.paperclip.ing/start/what-is-paperclip
https://docs.paperclip.ing/guides/agent-developer/heartbeat-protocol
https://docs.paperclip.ing/guides/board-operator/costs-and-budgets
https://docs.paperclip.ing/guides/board-operator/activity-log
https://docs.paperclip.ing/deploy/secrets
https://docs.paperclip.ing/adapters/overview
```

### Architecture

Paperclip’s architecture centers on a **control plane + agent runtimes** model:

* The official docs explicitly describe “Two Layers,” where Paperclip is the control plane managing agent registry, org chart, budgets, goal hierarchy, task assignment/status, and heartbeat monitoring. citeturn17view0turn17view3  
* Agents communicate via a standardized **heartbeat protocol** (“core contract”), which includes identity fetch, approval follow-up, assignments inbox retrieval, work selection, status updates, and delegation steps. citeturn17view3  
* **Adapters** are the bridge to real execution environments: on heartbeat, Paperclip resolves adapter type/config and calls the adapter’s `execute()` to spawn/call the agent runtime, parse outputs, and return structured results. citeturn17view5turn17view4  
* Paperclip ships built-in adapters to multiple common agent runtimes (Claude Code CLI, Codex CLI, Gemini CLI, OpenCode CLI, Cursor, OpenClaw gateway, etc.). citeturn17view5

This is materially different from Linear: Paperclip is not merely enabling third-party agents inside a PM tool—it is building a **meta-organization** of agents.

### Integrations

Paperclip’s integration layer is adapter-driven and extensible:

* Custom adapters can be created with a documented package structure spanning shared metadata, server execution logic, environment diagnostics/testing, UI modules, and CLI modules. citeturn17view4turn17view5  
* Paperclip’s GitHub README also references a plugin system roadmap item and a community plugin ecosystem. citeturn21view3turn19view0

In practice, Paperclip becomes the place you integrate **agent runtimes**. Integrating external “business systems” (VCS, CI/CD, chat) is typically achieved by giving agents tools/credentials and tracking those actions through Paperclip’s logging/tracing systems (see Observability + Secrets). citeturn21view2turn27view2

### Governance and security

Paperclip is the strongest of the four on explicit governance controls:

* **Approval gates**: hiring an agent can require board approval (`hire_agent` approvals include role/capabilities/config/budget), and the CEO strategy also requires approval before execution. citeturn5view6turn21view4  
* **Budget enforcement**: cost events are reported per heartbeat (provider/model/input/output/cost cents) and aggregated monthly; budgets are configurable at company and per-agent level; enforcement includes an 80% warning and a 100% hard stop that auto-pauses the agent (stopping heartbeats). citeturn17view1turn22view1  
* **Audit logging**: every mutation is recorded; the activity log is explicitly described as complete, filterable, and useful for debugging. The API docs also describe the activity log as append-only and immutable. citeturn21view0turn21view1  
* **Secrets management**: Paperclip encrypts secrets at rest using a local master key created at onboarding and stored locally; agent env vars with sensitive values can be stored as encrypted secret references, and strict mode enforces secret refs for sensitive keys. The server resolves/decrypts and injects secrets at runtime. citeturn21view2  
* **Authentication modes**: agents can receive short-lived run JWTs (via `PAPERCLIP_API_KEY`) scoped to agent + run; long-lived agent API keys can be created and are hashed at rest (full value shown only at creation). Board operators authenticate via session-based auth. citeturn17view2turn17view6  
* **Telemetry**: enabled by default but described as anonymous and excluding personal information, issue content, prompts, file paths, and secrets; it’s explicitly disable-able via env or config. citeturn21view3

### Developer experience

Paperclip’s developer surface is substantial:

* A REST API for control plane operations (JSON, `/api` base). citeturn17view6turn20search33  
* A CLI-first onboarding and operational toolset (`onboard`, `doctor`, `configure`), including explicit validation of secrets and storage configuration. citeturn21view5turn5view7  
* Adapter building and testing mechanics (environment test hooks; UI and CLI extension points). citeturn17view4turn17view5  
* A declared plugin direction in the project roadmap. citeturn21view3

### Observability and debugging

Paperclip’s observability is unusually explicit and multi-layered:

* **Heartbeats** are first-class execution units with a standardized step model, enabling consistent replay/debug practices. citeturn17view3  
* **Run history** is referenced as part of operational monitoring (“see each heartbeat execution on the agent detail page”). citeturn22view0  
* **Activity log** is positioned as the “first stop” for debugging unexpected behavior. citeturn21view0  
* The marketing site describes a “Ticket System” where “every tool call and decision” is recorded, with “full trace” and “immutable audit log.” citeturn27view2

### Cost model and licensing

Paperclip is open-source under the **MIT license**. citeturn21view3turn19view0 Operationally, however, it is designed to track and enforce the primary cost driver in agent systems: **token spend per provider/model**, tracked through heartbeat cost reporting and enforced through budgets. citeturn17view1turn17view3

### Setup and operational effort

Paperclip supports a “single-command” onboarding flow (`npx paperclipai onboard --yes`) and documents a quickstart mode with local defaults (embedded DB, local disk storage, default secrets) as well as advanced configuration. citeturn19view2turn21view5turn5view7 Operationally, it becomes more intensive as you harden deployments (authenticated mode, strict secrets, remote hosting), but Paperclip exposes explicit operational commands and health checks. citeturn21view5turn21view2

### Best-fit use cases and key limitations

Paperclip best fits teams (or “solo operators”) who want a **governable, auditable multi-agent organization** with strict cost control and layered autonomy. citeturn17view0turn17view1turn21view4turn27view2 Key risks are typical of self-hosted control planes: you own uptime, security hardening, secrets management posture, and agent runtime safety boundaries—even though Paperclip provides many primitives to manage those risks. citeturn21view2turn21view5turn17view5

## Multica.ai (multica)

### Purpose and positioning

Multica positions itself as an **“AI-native task management”** product that “turns coding agents into real teammates” and supports a hybrid workforce (humans + agent workers). citeturn12search3turn11view0 The GitHub README emphasizes eliminating prompt babysitting: agents “show up on the board,” claim tasks, and update statuses autonomously—explicitly highlighting compatibility with Claude Code and Codex. citeturn11view0turn13view2

**Primary sources (official):**
```text
https://multica.ai/
https://github.com/multica-ai/multica
https://github.com/multica-ai/multica/blob/main/SELF_HOSTING.md
https://github.com/multica-ai/multica/blob/main/CLI_AND_DAEMON.md
```

### Architecture

Multica’s architecture is unusually clear in its own documentation:

* The README includes an explicit architecture diagram: Next.js frontend + Go backend + PostgreSQL (pgvector) + local agent daemon. citeturn11view0  
* The Self-Hosting Guide describes three server-side components: Go backend (REST API + WebSocket server), Next.js 16 frontend, PostgreSQL 17 + pgvector database; plus a per-user CLI + daemon to run AI agents locally. citeturn18view0  
* The CLI/daemon is the runtime: it detects installed agent CLIs, registers them, polls for claimed tasks (default 3s), spawns the agent CLI in an isolated workspace directory, streams results back, and sends periodic heartbeats (default 15s). Logs are written to `~/.multica/daemon.log` by default. citeturn13view2  
* Supported agent CLIs are explicitly listed (Claude Code `claude`, Codex `codex`) and are auto-detected on PATH. citeturn13view2turn11view0

Deployment-wise, Multica supports both **hosted cloud** (“Start free trial”) and **self-hosting**. citeturn12search3turn11view0turn18view0

### Integrations

Multica’s “integration” story (based on primary sources captured here) is primarily: **agent runtime integration via CLIs**.

* The daemon lists Claude Code CLI and Codex CLI explicitly, and provides environment-variable configuration for daemon behavior and agent overrides. citeturn13view2  
* No first-party Slack/Jira/GitHub integration layer is documented in the extracted Multica official docs (beyond what agents can do on the local machine). Therefore, treat chat/CI/CD/VCS integrations as “agent-capability dependent,” not “platform-provided.” citeturn13view2turn11view0

### Governance and security

Multica documents authentication and some security-relevant deployment controls, but compared to Paperclip it exposes fewer explicit governance primitives:

* CLI login supports browser-based OAuth authentication and issues a 90-day personal access token; token-based login exists for headless environments. citeturn13view2turn8view4  
* Self-hosted authentication is email-based magic link auth via Resend, with optional Google OAuth, and uses a JWT secret for signing tokens. citeturn18view0  
* Multi-workspace isolation is presented as a first-class feature (“workspace-level isolation; each workspace has its own agents, issues, settings”). citeturn11view0  

What is *not* clearly documented (in the captured primary sources) are: RBAC models beyond workspace membership, approval workflows for risky actions, detailed audit log retention/format, and explicit budget controls. Those should be treated as **unknown/unspecified** rather than assumed.

### Developer experience

Multica is open-source and provides:

* A CLI for workspace management, issue tracking, and daemon runtime management. citeturn13view2  
* A self-hosting deployment guide (Docker Compose or build-from-source, environment-variable configuration, reverse proxy guidance). citeturn18view0  
* A “skills” concept described as reusable capabilities that compound over time. citeturn11view0  

However, the attempted opens for multica.ai “Documentation” / “API” pages returned errors in this research session (likely due to dynamic rendering or access restrictions), so any deeper API/SDK claims would be speculative and are intentionally avoided. citeturn12search3turn11view3

### Observability and debugging

Multica has multiple observability-like mechanisms at runtime level:

* Real-time progress streaming is described as via WebSocket in the README feature list. citeturn11view0  
* The daemon provides a status command (PID/uptime/detected agents/watched workspaces) and supports tailing logs; it also has explicit heartbeats so the server knows the daemon is alive. citeturn13view2

Compared with Paperclip’s full trace + immutable audit log posture, Multica’s documented observability is currently more “runtime operational telemetry” than “governance-grade audit trails.” citeturn13view2turn21view1turn27view2

### Cost model and licensing

Multica is open-source (Apache 2.0 license). citeturn12search1turn11view0 The hosted cloud advertises a free trial, but no official pricing details were found in the accessible sources used here; therefore, **hosted pricing is unknown**. citeturn12search3 Your actual operating cost will be dominated by compute for the server + the local runtimes and the underlying agent CLI/provider costs, but Multica’s docs in this session do not specify internal metering/budget enforcement analogous to Paperclip. citeturn11view0turn13view2

### Setup and operational effort

Self-hosting Multica requires a backend + frontend + PostgreSQL (with pgvector), and configuration via environment variables; notably, it also requires each user to install and run the `multica` daemon locally for executing tasks on their machine. citeturn18view0turn13view2 This yields a “distributed runtime fleet” operational model—powerful, but potentially heavier to manage for large orgs.

### Best-fit use cases and key limitations

Multica fits teams that want a **workboard** where coding agents behave like teammates and execution happens on developer machines or managed runtimes, with low friction and real-time progress streaming. citeturn11view0turn13view2 The biggest risk is maturity/coverage: enterprise governance features (approvals, audit logs, RBAC, budgets) are not clearly documented in the sources accessed here, and the local daemon model may be difficult to standardize across large fleets without additional tooling. citeturn18view0turn13view2

## Craft Agents

### Purpose and positioning

Craft Agents is positioned as an **open-source desktop app** for working with AI agents: “connect any API or MCP server,” multitask naturally, and “make it yours.” citeturn6search10turn9search27 The official docs describe it as extending Claude’s coding capabilities across your tools (GitHub, Linear, Slack, Craft docs, local files, and any API you connect). citeturn9search7turn16view2

**Primary sources (official):**
```text
https://agents.craft.do/
https://agents.craft.do/docs/getting-started/introduction
https://agents.craft.do/docs/core-concepts/permissions
https://agents.craft.do/docs/sources/overview
https://agents.craft.do/docs/automations/overview
https://agents.craft.do/docs/reference/config/credentials
https://github.com/lukilabs/craft-agents-oss
```

### Architecture

Craft Agents’ architecture is **agent-native desktop orchestration**:

* The open-source repo describes it as a desktop tool enabling multitasking, connecting to APIs/services, sharing sessions, and using a more document-centric workflow; it uses the Claude Agent SDK and Pi SDK “side by side.” citeturn8view3turn5view13  
* “Sources” are the core integration primitive and support three types: MCP servers, REST APIs, and local folders. Source configuration lives on disk in per-workspace folders (e.g., `~/.craft-agent/workspaces/{workspace-id}/sources/{source-slug}/` with `config.json`, optional `guide.md`, optional `permissions.json`). citeturn16view2  
* API sources create a flexible HTTP tool, handle auth via securely stored credentials, validate via test endpoint, then allow calls to any endpoint under a base URL. citeturn16view4  
* Automations provide event-driven execution: when events fire (labels/tools/cron schedules), Craft Agents runs command actions or prompt actions that can create new sessions; configuration lives in `automations.json`. citeturn16view1  

### Integrations

Craft Agents’ integration coverage is unusually broad because it is **tool-agnostic**:

* MCP servers: examples include Linear, GitHub, Brave Search; the docs show an example Linear MCP config using `https://mcp.linear.app` with OAuth. citeturn16view2turn5view2  
* REST APIs: any service with HTTP endpoints, with multiple auth types. citeturn16view4  
* Local folders: bookmarks to filesystem locations for agent access. citeturn16view2  
* The GitHub README lists “Sources” like REST APIs (Google, Slack, Microsoft) and MCP servers (Craft, Linear, GitHub, Notion) and also mentions a “Craft MCP Integration” with 32+ document tools. citeturn5view13

This makes Craft Agents strong on **integration breadth**, although the depth and safety of each integration depends heavily on permission configuration and the underlying MCP/API server semantics.

### Governance and security

Craft Agents provides governance primarily through local controls:

* **Permission modes**: Explore (read-only), Ask to Edit (prompts before edits), Execute (allow-all). Explore mode explicitly blocks writes, shell mutations, MCP mutations, and API mutation verbs. citeturn16view0  
* **Credential storage**: credentials are stored in an encrypted local file (`~/.craft-agent/credentials.enc`) using AES-256-GCM, with keys derived from machine-specific identifiers so the file can only be decrypted on the origin machine. citeturn27view0  
* **MCP local server isolation**: when spawning local MCP servers, Craft Agents filters out sensitive environment variables (API keys/tokens) to prevent leakage to subprocesses. citeturn8view1  
* **MCP auth**: supports OAuth (browser-based), bearer tokens, and no auth; OAuth tokens are stored for future use (exact storage details governed by the credential system above). citeturn16view3turn27view0  

What Craft Agents does **not** clearly claim (in accessible primary sources) is enterprise-grade centralized RBAC, audit log retention policies, or spend/budget enforcement. It is best understood as user-controlled, local-first governance augmented by automation and configuration. citeturn16view0turn27view0

### Developer experience

Craft Agents is open-source (Apache 2.0) with both one-line install and build-from-source workflows referenced in the repo. citeturn9search2turn8view3 Extensibility is more configuration-driven than SDK-driven: by adding Sources (MCP/API/local) and Automations, users effectively “program” the agent’s capabilities without writing a custom plugin system (though the codebase itself is forkable/remixable). citeturn16view2turn16view1turn6search10

### Observability and debugging

Craft Agents focuses on UX-level observability:

* Streaming responses, tool visualization, real-time updates, background tasks with progress tracking, a VS Code-style multi-file diff viewer, and configurable session statuses. citeturn5view13  
* Browser tools support screenshots and even “Inspect Network,” which can help debug web automations and discover internal endpoints. citeturn16view5  

### Cost model and licensing

Craft Agents is open-source under Apache 2.0. citeturn5view13turn6search2 Running it locally still requires model/provider access (Anthropic/OpenAI/Copilot/etc.), as the app supports multiple provider connections, but Craft Agents itself is not marketed with a SaaS seat price in the sources captured here. citeturn5view13turn16view2

### Setup and operational effort

Setup is typically lighter than server-centric platforms: install the desktop app, configure model connections, then add Sources and Automations. citeturn9search2turn16view2turn16view1 The docs also reference “Server & CLI” options (e.g., “Headless Server”), implying Craft Agents can be used beyond purely interactive desktop usage, but operational details were not fully explored here. citeturn16view4

### Best-fit use cases and key limitations

Craft Agents is best when you need a **high-integration, permissioned desktop agent workspace** that can operate across many tools via MCP/API/local context, and when you value “configuration by conversation” and local credential control. citeturn16view2turn16view0turn27view0 The main limitation is organizational scale: compared with Paperclip’s governance/budget/audit-first approach, Craft Agents is less of an enterprise control plane and more of an adaptable agent cockpit. citeturn27view2turn21view1

## Cross-platform comparison and buyer matrix

### Architectural comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  subgraph Linear[Linear: PM-native agents]
    L1[Linear SaaS Workspace] --> LAPI[GraphQL API + Webhooks]
    L1 --> LMCP[Linear MCP server]
    AgentApp[Agent Integration / App] -->|OAuth actor=app| LAPI
    MCPClient[MCP client (Claude/Cursor/etc.)] --> LMCP
  end

  subgraph Paperclip[Paperclip: control plane for agent companies]
    Board[Board Operator UI] --> PCS[Paperclip Control Plane]
    PCS --> DB1[(DB + state)]
    PCS -->|heartbeat| Adapter[Adapter execute()]
    Adapter --> Runtime[Agent runtime: Claude/Codex/etc.]
    PCS --> Audit[Immutable activity/audit trail]
    PCS --> Budget[Budgets + enforcement]
  end

  subgraph Multica[Multica: AI-native task management + daemon runtime]
    Web[Next.js Web UI] --> Go[Go REST + WebSocket]
    Go --> DB2[(Postgres + pgvector)]
    Daemon[Local daemon on user machine] -->|poll + run + heartbeats| Go
    Daemon --> CLI[Claude/Codex CLIs]
  end

  subgraph Craft[Craft Agents: desktop agent interface]
    Desktop[Desktop app] --> LLM[LLM providers]
    Desktop --> Src[Sources: MCP / API / Local folders]
    Src --> Tools[External systems: GitHub/Linear/Slack/etc.]
    Desktop --> Perm[Permission modes]
    Desktop --> Auto[Automations]
  end
```

The key distinction is **where execution authority lives**. Paperclip and Multica both formalize execution (heartbeats, daemon polling, task lifecycle) in ways that can run unattended. citeturn17view3turn13view2turn11view0 Craft Agents and Linear emphasize workflow-native collaboration, with stronger interactive affordances; Linear relies on agent providers for execution, and Craft Agents runs actions under user-governed permission modes and configuration. citeturn27view1turn16view0turn16view2turn15view0

### Decision heuristics

If your organization is already committed to Linear as the “system of record” for engineering work, **Linear agents** are the cleanest path to deploy agents without introducing a new task system, and the integration ecosystem is a major advantage. citeturn27view1turn15view1turn15view9

If you want a centralized, explicitly governed agent organization with **approvals, budgets, secrets, and immutable auditability**, Paperclip is the most governance-forward of the four, with the clearest control-plane model. citeturn21view4turn17view1turn21view2turn21view1turn27view2

If your goal is to manage a **fleet of coding agents as teammates** with execution occurring on developer machines (or cloud runtimes) and you can tolerate a newer product surface, Multica’s daemon + WebSocket model is purposeful. citeturn11view0turn13view2turn18view0

If you want the broadest “connect anything” experience with strong **interactive control via permission modes** and local encrypted credentials—especially for power users who orchestrate many tool connections—Craft Agents is compelling. citeturn16view2turn16view0turn27view0turn5view13

metadata.reasoning_title: Searching CI/CD, integrations, and LLM proxy support

parts:
Yep — adding those four dimensions makes the comparison a lot more useful.

## Updated compare: GitHub, CI/CD, stats, LLM proxy support

| Product | GitHub integration | CI/CD support | Public stats | LLM proxy / model gateway support |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Linear** | **Strong, first-party.** Official GitHub integration links PRs and commits to issues, syncs GitHub issues, review state, and automates workflow status changes. citeturn954114search1turn954114search2 | **Good for dev workflow orchestration, not a CI/CD engine.** It automates issue state from PR/commit activity and can sit on top of GitHub-based delivery, but it does not present itself as its own pipeline runner. citeturn954114search2turn954114search1 | **Closed-source product.** No main product repo, but Linear’s GitHub org shows 1.4k followers, and its main public tooling repo shows about 1.3k stars and 242 forks. citeturn566274view4 | **No platform-level LLM proxy.** Linear exposes APIs, webhooks, and an MCP server for agents, but it is not the runtime or model gateway itself. citeturn954114search10turn954114search4 |
| **Paperclip** | **Indirect / adapter-based.** It is designed to orchestrate agents and external systems via adapters such as HTTP, process, Codex, Cursor, Claude, etc., but I did not find a first-party GitHub integration doc comparable to Linear’s. citeturn954114search0turn954114search8 | **Partial.** It clearly supports deployment in the hosting sense and can orchestrate agents that do engineering work, but “Artifacts & Deployments” is still on the roadmap, so native CI/CD product support looks immature today. citeturn519055search2turn959574view0 | **Very large OSS footprint.** Repo shows **49.4k stars**, **8.1k forks**, **792 issues**, **1.1k PRs**, and **2,013 commits**. citeturn959574view0 | **Closest answer: BYO providers, not a dedicated proxy.** Paperclip says “any bot, any provider,” supports many adapters, and OpenCode is noted as multi-provider, but I did not find a documented OpenAI-compatible proxy/gateway layer as a first-class feature. citeturn331042search0turn954114search0turn959574view0 |
| **Multica** | **Weak / not clearly first-party.** The public site and docs emphasize coding-agent runtimes, local daemons, PR-oriented work, and skills, but I did not find a clear official GitHub integration page. citeturn519055search0turn566274view0 | **Moderate for engineering workflows, weak as native CI/CD.** Multica explicitly shows deploy-related skills like “Deploy to staging” and PR-centric workflows, but I did not find a built-in CI/CD product surface comparable to GitHub Actions or a dedicated deployment module. citeturn519055search0turn566274view0 | **Healthy OSS footprint.** Repo shows **2.3k stars**, **242 forks**, **31 issues**, **28 PRs**, and **1,842 commits**. citeturn566274view0turn566274view1 | **BYO provider/backends, but not clearly a proxy product.** Multica says “bring your own LLM provider,” “swap agent backends,” supports Claude Code and Codex, and shows cloud Anthropic plus local runtimes. That is strong backend flexibility, but not explicit proxy/gateway branding. citeturn519055search0turn519055search0 |
| **Craft Agents** | **Strong, but via sources rather than a built-in PM sync layer.** GitHub is a first-class source through MCP/API, and docs explicitly mention GitHub in workflows and attaching sessions to PRs. citeturn519055search1turn519055search3 | **Flexible, not native CI/CD.** Because it can connect to any REST API and run automations, it can likely drive CI/CD systems, but it is not itself described as a pipeline platform. citeturn519055search1turn519055search3 | **Solid OSS footprint.** Repo shows **3.6k stars**, **564 forks**, **178 issues**, **62 PRs**, and **72 commits**. citeturn566274view2turn566274view3 | **Best explicit support here.** Craft says all sources work with Anthropic, OpenAI/Codex, Gemini, GitHub Copilot, and **custom endpoints**. That is the clearest documented model-gateway / proxy-friendly story among the four. citeturn519055search1 |

## What changes from the earlier ranking

### Best GitHub integration
**Linear wins** if you care about issue/PR/commit/review sync as a managed workflow. Its GitHub integration is clearly the most productized and specific. citeturn954114search1turn954114search2

**Craft Agents** is next if you mean “my agent should use GitHub deeply,” because GitHub is a first-class source, but the pattern is more “agent connects to tools” than “PM system and GitHub are tightly synced.” citeturn519055search1turn519055search3

### Best CI/CD-adjacent support
None of these is really a classic CI/CD platform.

- **Linear** is strongest as the coordination layer around PR-driven delivery. citeturn954114search2
- **Craft Agents** is strongest if you want to wire arbitrary deployment APIs and automations together. citeturn519055search1turn519055search3
- **Multica** is strongest if CI/CD is embedded in agent skills and coding workflows. citeturn519055search0
- **Paperclip** looks more like orchestration/governance than DevOps-native delivery, and its own roadmap still lists deployments as future work. citeturn959574view0turn519055search2

### Best stats / momentum
Purely by public GitHub numbers:
1. **Paperclip** — by far the biggest OSS signal. citeturn959574view0
2. **Craft Agents** — sizable OSS community. citeturn566274view2
3. **Multica** — respectable, but smaller. citeturn566274view0
4. **Linear** — product is closed-source, so GitHub stats mainly reflect tooling and SDK ecosystem, not the product itself. citeturn566274view4

### Best LLM proxy / backend flexibility
**Craft Agents** is the clearest winner on documented multi-provider and custom endpoint support. citeturn519055search1

Then:
- **Multica**: good BYO-provider posture, but more runtime/backend than “proxy.” citeturn519055search0
- **Paperclip**: broad provider/runtime support via adapters, but again not explicitly a proxy gateway. citeturn954114search0turn331042search0
- **Linear**: not really in this category. citeturn954114search10

## My practical take

If your buying criteria now prioritize these four columns:

- Pick **Linear** for **GitHub + issue tracking + agent handoff**.
- Pick **Craft Agents** for **tool breadth + multi-provider flexibility + custom endpoints**.
- Pick **Multica** for **coding-agent teamwork with deploy/test skills inside the work loop**.
- Pick **Paperclip** for **governed orchestration**, not for polished GitHub/CI/CD plumbing.

## One caution
The biggest uncertainty is **native CI/CD** for Paperclip, Multica, and Craft. In all three cases, I found strong evidence they can participate in delivery workflows, but not the same level of first-party CI/CD documentation that Linear has for GitHub workflow sync. So I’d classify them as:

- **Linear:** documented workflow integration
- **Craft / Multica / Paperclip:** agent-driven or API-driven CI/CD enablement, but not clearly first-party CI/CD products citeturn954114search2turn519055search1turn519055search0turn959574view0

I can also turn this into a denser **buyer matrix** with scores from 1–5 for each column plus deployment model, governance, and pricing style.
